perturbation.original_text: Reseller shall comply with all applicable export and import control and other relevant laws of any applicable jurisdiction. Determination of the applicable law is Reseller's responsibility.
perturbation.changed_text: Reseller should try to be mindful of export and import control laws of any applicable jurisdiction. Determining the precise applicable law might be considered the Reseller's general guidance.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the Reseller's obligation to comply with export and import laws. Replacing 'shall comply' with 'should try to be mindful of' reduces the requirement to a suggestion. Additionally, changing 'Determination of the applicable law is Reseller's responsibility' to 'Determining the precise applicable law might be considered the Reseller's general guidance' makes the responsibility of determining applicable law ambiguous and less definitive.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Export and Import Permits Act (R.S.C., 1985, c. E-19)
perturbation.law_citation: Sections 13-17 of the Export and Import Permits Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Sections 13 to 17 of the Export and Import Permits Act outlines offenses and penalties for non-compliance with export and import regulations. By weakening the mandatory compliance to a mere mindfulness, the modified clause creates a contradiction because Canadian law requires strict adherence and accountability, not just a general awareness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Export and Import Permits Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu Softwood Lumber Products Export Access (continued) Marginal note: Export from a region 6.4 Ă An exported softwood lumber product is deemed to be exported from the region where the product underwent its first primary processing, as defined in section 2 of the Softwood Lumber Products Export Charge Act, 2006 . If, however, the exported product underwent its first primary processing in Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island, Newfoundland and Labrador, Yukon, the Northwest Territories or Nunavut from softwood sawlogs originating in a region, it is deemed to be exported from that region. 2006, c. 13, s. 111 Permits and Certificates Marginal note: Export permits 7 Ă (1) Ă Subject to subsection (2), the Minister may issue to any resident of Canada applying therefor a permit to export or transfer goods or technology included in an Export Control List or to export or transfer goods or technology to a country included in an Area Control List, in such quantity and of such quality, by such persons, to such places or persons and subject to such other terms and conditions as are described in the permit or in the regulations. (1.01) Ă [Repealed, 2018, c. 26, s. 6] Marginal note: General permits (1.1) Ă Notwithstanding subsection (1), the Minister may, by order, issue generally to all residents of Canada a general permit to export or transfer to any country specified in the permit any goods or technology included in an Export Control List that are specified in the permit, subject to such terms and conditions as are described in the permit. Marginal note: Export permit for automatic firearm (2) Ă The Minister may not issue a permit under subsection (1) to export any thing referred to in any of paragraphs 4.1(a) to (c), or any component or part of such a thing, that is included in an Export Control List unless (a) Ă the export is to a country included in an Automatic Firearms Country Control List; and (b) Ă the prohibited weapon or component or part thereof is exported to the government of, or a consignee authorized by the government of, that country. R.S., 1985, c. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the mandatory compliance with export and import laws, replacing 'shall comply' with 'should try to be mindful of.' This weakens the obligation. The original contract held the Reseller responsible for determining the applicable law, whereas the modified text softens this to 'Determining the precise applicable law might be considered the Reseller's general guidance,' making the responsibility ambiguous. The Law Explanation states that the Export and Import Permits Act outlines offenses and penalties for non-compliance, and that strict adherence is required, which the modified text contradicts. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Export and Import Permits Act' and refers to permits the 'Minister may issue... a permit to export or transfer goods or technology included in an Export Control List' which indicates that the importer/exporter is fully responsible for this aspect.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term and for seven (7) years after the expiration or termination of this Agreement, or such longer period as required by applicable law, Reseller agrees to maintain complete books, records and accounts relevant to the computation of and accounting for the amounts payable under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term and for a period that Reseller feels is reasonably adequate after the expiration or termination of this Agreement, Reseller will endeavor to maintain books, records and accounts that it deems relevant to the accounting of amounts payable under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the Reseller's obligation to maintain financial records for a defined period. Replacing 'seven (7) years after the expiration or termination of this Agreement, or such longer period as required by applicable law' with 'a period that Reseller feels is reasonably adequate after the expiration or termination of this Agreement' makes the record retention period subjective. Also, replacing 'maintain complete books, records and accounts relevant to the computation of and accounting for the amounts payable under this Agreement' with 'endeavor to maintain books, records and accounts that it deems relevant to the accounting of amounts payable under this Agreement' makes the scope of record-keeping subjective.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 230(4) of the Income Tax Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 230(4) of the Income Tax Act requires taxpayers to keep records and books of account, plus supporting documents, to enable an accurate determination of their tax obligations, and to retain those records for a period of six years from the end of the taxation year to which they relate. The modified text contradicts this by allowing the Reseller to subjectively determine the retention period and the relevance of the records, undermining the enforceability of tax obligations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the Reseller's responsibility to maintain records, contradicting the Income Tax Act's requirement for accurate tax determination. The original text specified a fixed period ('seven (7) years') and clear criteria ('complete books, records and accounts relevant to the computation of and accounting for the amounts payable'). In contrast, the changed text makes both the retention period and the scope of records subjective ('a period that Reseller feels is reasonably adequate' and 'books, records and accounts that it deems relevant'), undermining the enforceability of tax obligations, as stated in the Law Explanation.
